content.text: Most AI agent tasks complete in seconds: a single LLM call, one tool invocation, a quick summary. But a meaningful category of agent workloads cannot finish within the lifetime of a single HTTP request or even a single process run. Research agents that crawl hundreds of pages, code agents that iterate over large codebases, data-pipeline agents that process millions of records, or workflow agents that must wait days for human approval — all of these require an execution model that persists state, tolerates interruptions, and resumes correctly after restarts. This document covers the design principles, implementation patterns, and operational concerns for long-running agents.

A long-running agent is an autonomous process that executes multi-step reasoning and action loops over an extended time horizon — from minutes to hours, days, or longer. Unlike a request-response agent that returns a single answer, a long-running agent maintains a persistent execution context: accumulated memory, tool-call history, intermediate artifacts, and progress state. Each step in the loop — plan, act, observe, checkpoint — builds on the results of prior steps.

: the agent's progress is persisted to external storage at regular checkpoints, so that a crash, deployment, or infrastructure preemption does not force the work to restart from scratch. The agent resumes from its last checkpoint, re-executing only the steps that had not yet completed. This is the key distinction between a long-running agent and simply running a Python script for a long time — the latter has no durability guarantee.

Long-running agents typically run as background workers, scheduled jobs, or queue consumers — not as synchronous API handlers. The client that initiated the task receives a job ID immediately and polls or subscribes to status updates while the agent works asynchronously in the background.

The shift from short-lived to long-running agents unlocks an entirely different category of automation. A request-response agent is fundamentally limited by timeout constraints (typically 30–120 seconds for HTTP APIs), token-context limits that prevent accumulating large amounts of history, and the absence of any mechanism to handle human approval steps or external wait conditions.

Consider a due-diligence agent that must: search and read 50 recent filings, extract key financial metrics, cross-reference against a company database, draft a structured report, and route it for human review before sending. This workflow naturally spans 15–40 minutes of LLM computation plus unpredictable human latency. Without a long-running agent pattern, each restart would re-read every filing from scratch, wasting LLM tokens and API quota while introducing inconsistency between iterations.

workflows where the agent pauses at a decision gate — awaiting a human approval, a file upload, or an external event — then resumes exactly where it left off. This is impractical without durable state.
A crash or deployment mid-task loses only the current step, not the entire run. The agent reloads its checkpoint and continues from where it stopped.
Tasks with hundreds of steps, external waits, or cumulative context windows become feasible. The agent is not bound by a single HTTP timeout or a single LLM context window.
The agent can pause at approval gates, await feedback, or wait for external events (file upload, payment confirmation) before resuming.
Completed steps are not re-executed after a resume, so LLM tokens, API quota, and compute are not wasted on re-doing already-finished work.
Because each step's inputs, outputs, and state transitions are persisted, you get a full audit trail of what the agent did, when, and why — essential for regulated industries.
Fan-out to many sub-tasks (each with its own durable state) and fan-in to aggregate results, all coordinated by the orchestrator without a synchronous connection remaining open.
Durable state requires an external store (Redis, Postgres, S3, or a purpose-built durable execution engine). Managing this store — backups, migrations, retention — adds operational overhead.

Every step that has side effects (database writes, API calls, emails sent) must be idempotent or guarded by a deduplication key, because the same step may be attempted more than once after a crash and resume.

An agent that has been running for hours and is stuck on step 47 is harder to debug than a single function call that returns an error immediately. Distributed tracing and structured logging become essential.
If the checkpoint schema changes between deployments, in-flight agents may fail to deserialize their saved state. Schema versioning and migration strategies are mandatory.

Long-running agents accumulate context, history, and artifacts. Without explicit pruning or summarization strategies, memory usage grows unboundedly, eventually degrading performance or exceeding LLM token limits.
The client receives no synchronous result — only a job ID. UX patterns must accommodate asynchronous workflows with status polling or push notifications.
A long-running agent operates as a

. At each iteration, the agent calls an LLM with the accumulated context to decide the next action; executes that action (a tool call, code execution, API request, or wait); observes the result; and then persists its state to an external store before proceeding to the next iteration. The checkpoint write is the critical safety boundary: once it succeeds, the step is considered durably complete.

The loop terminates when one of several conditions is met: the LLM signals task completion, a predefined goal is satisfied, a maximum step count or time budget is exhausted, or an unrecoverable error is encountered. Termination conditions must be explicitly designed — without them, the agent may loop indefinitely.
On restart after a crash, the agent loads the latest checkpoint and replays only the steps that had not yet checkpointed. This means every step must be

(or the replayed result must be cached from the previous run). Nondeterminism — such as using the current timestamp, random seeds, or reading live data that has since changed — causes the resumed run to diverge from the original, potentially invalidating prior steps.

The checkpoint frequency is a trade-off between durability and overhead. Checkpointing after every step maximizes safety but incurs a write to the state store on each iteration. Checkpointing every N steps reduces I/O but means up to N steps of work are lost on a crash. A common middle ground is to checkpoint after every LLM call, since LLM API latency (several seconds) dominates over the negligible cost of a database write.

attrs.alt: Long-Running Agent Architecture: Client triggers Agent Orchestrator (plan, execute, observe, checkpoint loop) which saves to State Store and dispatches to Task Queue, while calling External Tools. Fault tolerance timeline shows checkpoint-and-resume after crash.

content.text: The architecture separates four concerns: the
manages worker dispatch and retries; and
provide the capabilities the agent acts on. This separation allows each component to be scaled, replaced, or maintained independently.
Long-running agent architecture is appropriate when one or more of the following conditions apply:
, which is the typical maximum for synchronous HTTP handlers or serverless functions.
The task involves dozens or hundreds of steps
that accumulate context and artifacts across iterations.
The workflow includes human approval gates
or external wait conditions (file upload, payment, external API callback) where the agent must pause indefinitely.
— if the agent completes 80 out of 100 steps, that partial result is useful and should not be discarded on failure.
— cloud spot instances, preemptible VMs, container orchestrators that evict pods, or CI environments with time limits.

Do not use this pattern for tasks that complete reliably in under 10 seconds and have no human-in-the-loop component. The state management overhead and asynchronous UX introduce complexity that is not justified for short-lived agents.

The following steps describe building a long-running agent from scratch using Python, Redis for state storage, and a simple job queue. Later sections show how purpose-built frameworks like Temporal simplify this considerably.

Decide what information must be persisted between steps: the current step index, accumulated results, tool call history, and any intermediate artifacts. Use a versioned dataclass or Pydantic model so you can migrate in-flight agents when the schema changes.
Implement the checkpoint save and load functions.
Serialize the state to JSON and write it to an external store (Redis, Postgres, S3). Tag each checkpoint with a schema version number. On resume, deserialize and validate.
The loop calls the LLM with the current context, executes the chosen tool, appends the result to state, checkpoints, and checks the termination condition.
Before executing a tool, check the state's tool call log: if this exact call (same tool, same arguments) was already completed in a prior run, return the cached result instead of re-executing.
Wrap the loop in a background worker or queue consumer.
The agent should run asynchronously as a background job. The client receives a job ID, polls a status endpoint, or subscribes to a webhook for completion.
Add a heartbeat or progress reporting mechanism.
Update a "last seen" timestamp in the state store on every checkpoint. A monitoring process can detect stalled agents (no heartbeat for N minutes) and alert or retry.
As the agent accumulates step results, the LLM context window fills. Summarize older steps using the LLM itself, keeping only recent history and critical artifacts in the active context.

import json
import time
from dataclasses import dataclass, field, asdict
from typing import Any
import redis
import anthropic

# --- State schema ---

@dataclass
class AgentState:
    schema_version: int = 1
    job_id: str = ""
    goal: str = ""
    step_index: int = 0
    max_steps: int = 30
    results: list[dict] = field(default_factory=list)
    completed: bool = False
    error: str | None = None

# --- Checkpoint helpers ---

def save_checkpoint(r: redis.Redis, state: AgentState) -> None:
    key = f"agent:{state.job_id}:state"
    r.set(key, json.dumps(asdict(state)), ex=86400)  # TTL: 24 hours

def load_checkpoint(r: redis.Redis, job_id: str) -> AgentState | None:
    key = f"agent:{job_id}:state"
    data = r.get(key)
    if data is None:
        return None
    return AgentState(**json.loads(data))

# --- Agent loop ---

def run_agent(job_id: str, goal: str, redis_url: str = "redis://localhost:6379") -> str:
    r = redis.from_url(redis_url)
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()

    # Resume from checkpoint if available, else start fresh
    state = load_checkpoint(r, job_id) or AgentState(job_id=job_id, goal=goal)

    while not state.completed and state.step_index < state.max_steps:
        # Build context from accumulated results
        context = f"Goal: {state.goal}\n\nProgress so far:\n"
        for r_item in state.results[-10:]:  # Keep last 10 to limit tokens
            context += f"Step {r_item['step']}: {r_item['summary']}\n"

        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=512,
            messages=[
                {
                    "role": "user",
                    "content": (
                        f"{context}\n\nWhat is the next step to complete the goal? "
                        "If the goal is complete, reply with DONE: followed by a summary."
                    )
                }
            ]
        )
        reply = response.content[0].text

        if reply.startswith("DONE:"):
            state.completed = True
            state.results.append({"step": state.step_index, "summary": reply[5:].strip()})
        else:
            # Simulate tool execution (replace with real tool calls)
            state.results.append({"step": state.step_index, "summary": reply[:200]})
            state.step_index += 1

        # Checkpoint after every step
        save_checkpoint(r, state)
        time.sleep(0.5)  # Rate-limit guard

    if not state.completed:
        state.error = "Max steps reached without completing goal"
        save_checkpoint(r, state)

    return state.results[-1]["summary"] if state.results else "No result"

import hashlib
import json
from typing import Callable, Any

def make_tool_key(tool_name: str, args: dict) -> str:
    """Create a stable cache key for a tool invocation."""
    payload = json.dumps({"tool": tool_name, "args": args}, sort_keys=True)
    return hashlib.sha256(payload.encode()).hexdigest()[:16]

def execute_tool_idempotent(
    tool_name: str,
    args: dict,
    tool_fn: Callable[..., Any],
    tool_cache: dict[str, Any],  # persisted as part of agent state
) -> Any:
    """
    Execute a tool only if it has not already been run with these exact arguments.
    Returns the cached result on retry, preventing double-writes or duplicate API calls.
    """
    key = make_tool_key(tool_name, args)
    if key in tool_cache:
        print(f"[cache hit] {tool_name}({args}) -> returning cached result")
        return tool_cache[key]

    result = tool_fn(**args)
    tool_cache[key] = result  # Cache before returning; state is checkpointed next
    return result


# Usage example
def send_email(to: str, subject: str, body: str) -> str:
    """Real email send — must not be called twice for the same step."""
    # ... actual email sending logic ...
    return f"Email sent to {to}"

tool_cache: dict[str, Any] = {}  # Loaded from / saved to checkpoint

result = execute_tool_idempotent(
    "send_email",
    {"to": "user@example.com", "subject": "Report ready", "body": "Your report is attached."},
    send_email,
    tool_cache,
)
# If the agent crashes immediately after the tool call but before checkpointing,
# on the next run the same call is a cache hit — the email is not re-sent.
Context window management via rolling summary

import anthropic

MAX_ACTIVE_STEPS = 8  # Keep only the last N steps in context

def summarize_steps(steps: list[dict], client: anthropic.Anthropic) -> str:
    """
    Summarize a batch of earlier steps into a compact description.
    This prevents the context window from filling up on long-running tasks.
    """
    if not steps:
        return ""
    steps_text = "\n".join(f"Step {s['step']}: {s['summary']}" for s in steps)
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5",
        max_tokens=300,
        messages=[
            {
                "role": "user",
                "content": (
                    f"Summarize the following completed agent steps in 2-3 sentences, "
                    f"preserving the key facts and outcomes:\n\n{steps_text}"
                )
            }
        ]
    )
    return response.content[0].text

def build_context(results: list[dict], archived_summary: str, client: anthropic.Anthropic) -> tuple[str, str]:
    """
    Maintain a rolling window of recent steps. Older steps are summarized and archived.
    Returns (active_context, updated_archive).
    """
    if len(results) <= MAX_ACTIVE_STEPS:
        active = results
        archive = archived_summary
    else:
        # Summarize the oldest steps that overflow the window
        overflow = results[:-MAX_ACTIVE_STEPS]
        new_summary = summarize_steps(overflow, client)
        archive = (archived_summary + "\n" + new_summary).strip()
        active = results[-MAX_ACTIVE_STEPS:]

    context_parts = []
    if archive:
        context_parts.append(f"[Earlier progress summary]\n{archive}")
    context_parts.extend(
        f"Step {s['step']}: {s['summary']}" for s in active
    )
    return "\n\n".join(context_parts), archive

Consider a competitive intelligence agent that monitors a list of 30 competitor websites and generates a weekly summary report. The agent must: fetch each competitor's changelog and blog, extract product announcements from the last 7 days using the LLM, deduplicate and cluster related announcements, draft a structured Markdown report, and email it to a distribution list. The full run takes 20–35 minutes for 30 competitors, involves 90–150 LLM calls, and must be restartable if a request times out or a scraping target blocks the IP.

import json
import time
from dataclasses import dataclass, field, asdict
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
import redis
import anthropic
import httpx

@dataclass
class CompIntelState:
    schema_version: int = 2
    job_id: str = ""
    competitors: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    processed: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)  # Competitors done
    announcements: dict[str, list[str]] = field(default_factory=dict)  # competitor -> items
    report_draft: str = ""
    email_sent: bool = False
    started_at: str = ""

STATE_TTL = 7 * 86400  # 7-day TTL; job runs weekly

def save(r: redis.Redis, state: CompIntelState) -> None:
    r.set(f"compintel:{state.job_id}", json.dumps(asdict(state)), ex=STATE_TTL)
    r.set(f"compintel:{state.job_id}:heartbeat", time.time(), ex=300)

def load(r: redis.Redis, job_id: str) -> CompIntelState | None:
    data = r.get(f"compintel:{job_id}")
    return CompIntelState(**json.loads(data)) if data else None

def fetch_competitor_content(url: str) -> str:
    """Fetch and return raw text content from a URL."""
    try:
        response = httpx.get(url, follow_redirects=True, timeout=15)
        response.raise_for_status()
        return response.text[:8000]  # Limit to 8 KB to stay within token budget
    except Exception as e:
        return f"[fetch error: {e}]"

def extract_announcements(content: str, competitor: str, client: anthropic.Anthropic) -> list[str]:
    """Use LLM to extract product announcements from raw page content."""
    cutoff = (datetime.utcnow() - timedelta(days=7)).strftime("%Y-%m-%d")
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=600,
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": (
                f"Extract product announcements, feature releases, and major updates "
                f"from the following content for {competitor} that were published after {cutoff}. "
                f"Return as a JSON list of strings (empty list if none found).\n\n{content}"
            )
        }]
    )
    try:
        return json.loads(response.content[0].text)
    except json.JSONDecodeError:
        return []

def run_compintel_agent(
    job_id: str,
    competitors: list[str],
    recipient_email: str,
    redis_url: str = "redis://localhost:6379"
) -> str:
    r = redis.from_url(redis_url)
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()

    state = load(r, job_id) or CompIntelState(
        job_id=job_id,
        competitors=competitors,
        started_at=datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
    )

    # Phase 1: Process each competitor (skip already done)
    for competitor_url in state.competitors:
        if competitor_url in state.processed:
            continue  # Resume: skip already-processed competitors

        content = fetch_competitor_content(competitor_url)
        items = extract_announcements(content, competitor_url, client)
        state.announcements[competitor_url] = items
        state.processed.append(competitor_url)
        save(r, state)  # Checkpoint after each competitor
        time.sleep(1)  # Polite crawl delay

    # Phase 2: Draft the report (skip if already drafted)
    if not state.report_draft:
        all_items = [
            f"{url}: {item}"
            for url, items in state.announcements.items()
            for item in items
        ]
        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=2000,
            messages=[{
                "role": "user",
                "content": (
                    "Write a concise competitive intelligence weekly summary as a Markdown report "
                    f"covering the following announcements:\n\n" + "\n".join(all_items)
                )
            }]
        )
        state.report_draft = response.content[0].text
        save(r, state)

    # Phase 3: Send email (idempotent — skip if already sent)
    if not state.email_sent:
        # send_email(recipient_email, "Weekly Competitive Intelligence", state.report_draft)
        state.email_sent = True
        save(r, state)
        print(f"Report emailed to {recipient_email}")

    return state.report_draft
If the agent runs for 20 minutes and checkpoints only on completion, a crash at minute 19 restarts the entire job. Checkpoint after every step or every LLM call.
An agent that sends an email or writes to a database on every retry without a deduplication guard will produce duplicate effects. Cache or idempotency-key every side-effecting tool call.
Storing the full context window in the checkpoint.

After 50 steps, the active context may be 20,000 tokens. Serializing and deserializing this on every checkpoint is slow and produces enormous state payloads. Store only structured step results; reconstruct the LLM messages array from them at resume time.
An agent without a step limit or time budget can loop forever if the LLM never converges on a termination signal. Always enforce a hard cap on iterations and elapsed time.
Renaming a field in the checkpoint dataclass without migration logic causes deserialization errors on resume. Version the schema explicitly and write a migration function for every breaking change.
An agent that crashes after step 3 with no monitoring can sit silently in a "running" state for days with no alert. Track a heartbeat timestamp and alert when it goes stale.
Use a purpose-built durable execution framework

(Temporal, Inngest, Restate) for production agents rather than rolling your own checkpoint logic. These frameworks handle state persistence, retries, timeouts, and versioning correctly and have been battle-tested at scale.
Instead of overwriting a single state blob, append each step's result to an event log. This makes debugging, replay, and audit straightforward, and avoids race conditions on concurrent writes.

The LLM call (plan) and the tool invocation (execute) should be distinct, checkpointed steps. If the tool call fails, you can retry it without re-consulting the LLM; if the LLM call fails, you can retry it without re-executing side effects.
Log every step as a structured event with job ID, step index, tool name, latency, and outcome. This enables dashboards, latency analysis, and failure-rate tracking without custom instrumentation.
Use exponential backoff with jitter for tool retries.
Transient failures (rate limits, network timeouts) are common. Retry with backoff rather than failing the entire job immediately. Log retried attempts for debugging.

Each job should have a maximum token budget, maximum step count, and maximum wall-clock duration. When any limit is reached, checkpoint the partial result and fail gracefully rather than running indefinitely.
The primary performance concern for long-running agents is

. If checkpointing takes 200ms per step and the agent runs 200 steps, 40 seconds of total runtime is spent on checkpoint writes. Use a fast in-memory store (Redis) for checkpoint writes and a slower durable store (Postgres, S3) for archival copies. Write to Redis synchronously and flush to the durable store asynchronously.

Context window management becomes a scaling bottleneck after roughly 50 steps. Each LLM call grows linearly in cost as the context accumulates. Strategies to control this: (1) summarize old steps with a cheaper model like claude-haiku, keeping only the summary in context; (2) use a retrieval-augmented approach where step results are stored in a vector database and only the top-k relevant results are injected into the current prompt; (3) store artifacts (files, database records) externally and reference them by ID in the context, rather than embedding their full content.

For horizontal scalability, run multiple agent workers in parallel against the same task queue. Each worker picks up an independent job; they do not share state. Parallelism is bounded by the LLM provider's rate limit, not by the agent infrastructure itself. If a single job has parallelizable sub-tasks (e.g., processing 100 documents independently), implement fan-out: the orchestrator spawns 100 child jobs, each with its own durable state, and polls for their completion before merging results.
Workflow-as-code, automatic replay, signals, child workflows, cron
Production multi-step agents with complex branching
Next.js / serverless agents with low infra overhead
Mature Python ecosystem, retry policies, scheduled tasks
Python shops needing simple async task execution
Async Python agents needing simple job queues
Journal-based, GRPC-native, low-latency, supports virtual objects
DAG-based, rich UI, artifact tracking, data-aware scheduling
ETL / data-pipeline agents with structured DAGs
Graph-based agent state machine, built-in persistence, streaming
LangChain users building stateful multi-step agents
Production agents requiring minimal operational code

Long-running agents introduce security concerns that do not apply to short-lived request handlers. Because the agent runs for extended periods with accumulated context and broad tool permissions, the attack surface is larger and the consequence of a compromise is proportionally greater.

An agent that reads external content (web pages, emails, documents) and injects it into the LLM context is vulnerable to adversarial content that redirects the agent's actions. Sanitize external content before injecting it into prompts; consider a secondary LLM call that extracts only structured data before feeding it to the orchestrator.
Grant the agent only the tool permissions required for the specific task. An agent that must read a database should not have write access. Scope API keys to the minimum necessary endpoints.
State blobs may contain API keys, PII, or sensitive business data accumulated during the run. Encrypt checkpoint data at rest using the storage provider's encryption or an application-layer key.

Each job should be associated with the identity of the initiating user. When the agent resumes, verify that the job's credentials are still valid (API keys not rotated, user account not deprovisioned) before proceeding.

A runaway agent that loops indefinitely can exhaust LLM API quotas or incur unbounded costs. Enforce per-job token budgets and per-job cost ceilings at the infrastructure level, not only in the agent code.

Jobs that failed without cleanup leave orphaned state in the store. Set TTLs on all checkpoint keys and run a periodic cleanup job that marks stale jobs (no heartbeat for 24 hours) as failed and removes their state after the retention window expires.
A long-running agent is defined by durable state: progress is checkpointed to external storage after each step so the agent can resume from where it stopped after any failure or restart.
The plan-act-observe-checkpoint loop is the core execution model. The checkpoint write after each iteration is the safety boundary that converts a fragile script into a durable agent.

Every tool call with a side effect must be idempotent or guarded by a deduplication key. On resume, the agent re-attempts only the step that failed — but that step may re-invoke the same tool if it was not cached.

Context window growth is the dominant scaling bottleneck. Use rolling summarization (summarize older steps with a cheaper model) or retrieval-augmented context injection to keep LLM input costs linear rather than quadratic.

Purpose-built durable execution frameworks (Temporal, Inngest, Restate) are strongly preferred over hand-rolled checkpoint logic for production systems. They handle replay, schema evolution, retries, and timing correctly.

Security surface increases with run duration: prompt injection via tool output, stale credentials, accumulated sensitive data in state, and runaway cost are the primary risks to address in long-running agent deployments.

Always enforce hard limits: maximum step count, token budget, and wall-clock timeout. These are safety nets that prevent infinite loops and unbounded API costs regardless of what the LLM decides to do.
